context.description: A German philosophical prose passage argues about the limits of comparing waking life and dreaming when discussing a hypothetical higher state of consciousness. It stresses the continuity of time, the physical vulnerability of sleep, and the inadequacy of analogies, then opens the section "Unmögliche Blicke" and introduces psychoactive substances as additional evidence.
normalization_notes: Page-level extract from a nonfiction philosophy book on consciousness.

rows.paragraph_text: The passage argues that dreaming cannot be the only state of consciousness if waking life is the source of dream content, and questions how a higher self or "above" state could exist without being lived through in time. It adds that sleep leaves the body physically vulnerable and that comparisons between dreaming and waking can only serve as partial analogies.
rows.text_content: The passage argues that dreaming cannot be the only state of consciousness if waking life is the source of dream content, and questions how a higher self or "above" state could exist without being lived through in time. It adds that sleep leaves the body physically vulnerable and that comparisons between dreaming and waking can only serve as partial analogies.

rows.paragraph_text: The author shifts to a cautious but forward-moving stance, warning that perceptions may be mirages of the brain while urging humility and continued inquiry behind the veil. The next topic introduces psychoactive substances as further clues.
rows.text_content: The author shifts to a cautious but forward-moving stance, warning that perceptions may be mirages of the brain while urging humility and continued inquiry behind the veil. The next topic introduces psychoactive substances as further clues.
